questions.specification: Which of the following best explains why an alloy, such as bronze or brass, is typically harder than a pure metal?

options.content: The alloying process removes delocalized electrons, which strengthens the electrostatic attraction between ions.
The introduction of different-sized atoms distorts the regular lattice, making it more difficult for layers of atoms to slide over each other.
Chemical reactions between the metals create a rigid network of covalent bonds that prevents deformation.
The different metals form a protective oxide layer inside the structure that acts as a physical barrier to movement.

questions.specification: Which of the following is the primary alloying element in stainless steel that provides its corrosion resistance?

options.content: Copper
Chromium
Nickel
Carbon

questions.specification:
Metallic alloys are frequently engineered to exhibit specific mechanical properties that pure metals lack. The diagram below illustrates the differences in atomic arrangement between a pure metal and one of its alloys.

![atomic structure](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-4c04b7f6-abab-41d2-ab7a-a7bc9ac9574b-1771929956947/36585548-96a8-436b-80c2-da48623040e7.jpeg)

Which of the following correctly explains why the structural change shown in the alloy diagram makes the material harder than the pure metal?

options.content: The presence of atoms of different sizes disrupts the regular arrangement, preventing the layers from sliding over each other easily.
The addition of different elements increases the concentration of delocalised electrons, which creates much stronger ionic attractions.
The alloying elements form covalent cross-links between the atoms of the host metal, locking them into a fixed 3D framework.
The larger atoms create gaps in the lattice that act as buffers, allowing the material to absorb more energy before it breaks.

questions.specification:
The grain size in a metal can affect the properties. Which of the following statements are true?

I. The larger the grains, the more brittle the metal.

II. The smaller the grains, the more brittle the metal.

III. The larger the grains, the more ductile the metal.

options.content: I only
options.markscheme: Statement I is false: larger grains generally allow easier dislocation motion, increasing ductility rather than brittleness.

options.content: II only
options.markscheme: Statement II is false: smaller grains generally increase strength/hardness (via grain boundary strengthening) and are not typically described as making the metal more brittle in this context.

options.content: III only
options.markscheme: Statement III is true: larger grains mean fewer grain boundaries to impede dislocation movement, so the metal tends to be more ductile.

options.content: I, II and III
options.markscheme: Not all statements are true: only Statement III is true, while Statements I and II are false.

questions.specification:
The table below shows two distinct stages in the production of a silver-alloy component.

| Stage | Process | Impact on Material Properties |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mechanical deformation (Cold-working) | Grains become elongated; the material becomes harder and more brittle. |
| 2 | Controlled heating and slow cooling | New grains form (recrystallization); the material becomes more ductile. |

Which heat treatment process is used in Stage 2 to restore the workability of the alloy?

options.content: Tempering
Case hardening
Annealing
Quenching

questions.specification: Which statement correctly identifies the effect of cold-rolling a metal sheet at room temperature on its mechanical properties?

options.content: The metal becomes more ductile as internal stresses are removed.
The metal's yield strength increases due to an increased density of dislocations.
The metal becomes softer as the crystal grains are enlarged.
The metal's thermal conductivity increases as its density decreases.
